revision.comment: 「[[宮中]]」を「[[宮中 (曖昧さ回避)]]」へ移動: 一般的に宮中に関する項目を作成するため
revision.text: #転送 [[宮中 (曖昧さ回避)]]

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''宮中'''（きゅうちゅう）とは、[[君主]]がいる[[宮廷]]の中のこと、後に[[政府]]の中を意味する'''[[府中]]'''が国政そのものを意味するようになったのに対して、宮廷内部の事務・君主の家政を意味するようになった。
== 戦前日本における「宮中」 == 戦前の日本における[[天皇]]は国家の最高統治者であるとともに[[国家神道]]の最高祭司でもあり、[[皇族]]の[[家長]]でもあった。そのため、[[宮内省]]（[[宮内大臣]]）、[[内大臣府]]（[[内大臣]]）、[[元老]]・[[重臣]]、[[侍従長]]、[[侍従武官長]]・[[枢密顧問官]]などといった天皇の日常及び各職務の遂行のために必要な組織・人員が置かれていたが、[[大日本帝国憲法]]において天皇の親裁権の確立と宮中と府中の分離という原則が並立していた。だが、一方で天皇が有していた[[天皇大権]]の大きさを背景として、国政の重要問題に関して天皇による調整が期待されるようになると、宮中側の人々も天皇制の維持・強化の方向において政治的な問題にも関与するようになった。特に[[昭和期]]には彼らは「'''宮中グループ'''」もしくは「'''宮廷グループ'''」と呼ばれ、軍部や官僚などの諸勢力と結びついて国政の動向に重要な影響を与えるようになった。だが、宮中グループも標的とされた[[二・二六事件]]以後は強大化する軍部の力を府中・宮中ともに抑制することが出来なくなったために、彼らと妥協もしくは積極的に加担することで自らの地位を保持するようになっていった。[[近衛文麿]]や[[木戸幸一]]はその代表的人物とされる。戦後、天皇から国政の実質的権限が剥奪され、国家神道も解体されたことにより、[[日本国憲法]]が認めた[[国事行為]]を行うのに必要な最低限必要な組織として[[宮内府]]→[[宮内庁]]が設置された。
== 参考文献 == *増田知子「宮中」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） *藤原彰「宮中グループ」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6）

== 参考文献 == *[[増田知子]]「宮中」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） *[[藤原彰]]「宮中グループ」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6） *[[茶谷誠一]]『昭和戦前期の宮中勢力と政治』（吉川弘文館、2009年） ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1）

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''宮中'''（きゅうちゅう）とは、[[天皇]]がいる[[宮廷]]の中のこと。後に[[政府]]の中を意味する'''[[府中]]'''が[[国政]]そのものを意味するようになったのに対して、宮廷内部の[[事務]]・[[君主]]の[[家政]]を意味するようになった。

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''宮中'''（きゅうちゅう）とは、[[宮殿]]の中のこと。<ref>[http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD デジタル大辞林]</ref>
== 日本における「宮中」 == 後に[[政府]]の中を意味する'''[[府中]]'''が[[日本の政治|国政]]そのものを意味するようになったのに対して、宮廷内部の[[事務]]・[[皇室]]の[[家政]]を意味するようになった。
[[戦前]]における[[天皇]]は[[国家]]の最高[[統治]]者であるとともに[[国家神道]]の最高[[祭司]]でもあり、[[皇族]]の[[家長]]でもあった。そのため、[[宮内省]]（[[宮内大臣]]）、[[内大臣府]]（[[内大臣]]）、[[元老]]・[[重臣]]、[[侍従長]]、[[侍従武官長]]・[[枢密顧問官]]などといった天皇の日常及び各職務の遂行のために必要な[[組織 (社会科学)|組織]]・人員が置かれていたが、[[大日本帝国憲法]]において天皇の[[親裁権]]の確立と宮中と府中の分離という原則が並立していた。だが、一方で天皇が有していた[[天皇大権]]の大きさを背景として、国政の重要問題に関して天皇による調整が期待されるようになると、宮中側の人々も天皇制の維持・強化の方向において[[政治]]的な問題にも関与するようになった。特に[[昭和期]]には彼らは「'''宮中グループ'''」もしくは「'''宮廷グループ'''」と呼ばれ、[[日本軍|軍部]]や[[官僚]]などの諸勢力と結びついて国政の動向に重要な影響を与えるようになった。だが、宮中グループも標的とされた[[二・二六事件]]以後は強大化する軍部の力を府中・宮中ともに抑制することが出来なくなったために、彼らと妥協もしくは積極的に加担することで自らの[[地位]]を保持するようになっていった。[[近衛文麿]]や[[木戸幸一]]はその代表的人物とされる。[[戦後]]、天皇から国政の実質的権限が剥奪され、国家神道も解体されたことにより、[[日本国憲法]]が認めた[[国事行為]]を行うのに必要な最低限必要な組織として[[宮内府]]→[[宮内庁]]が設置された。
== 参考文献 == *[[増田知子]]「宮中」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） *[[藤原彰]]「宮中グループ」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6） *[[茶谷誠一]]『昭和戦前期の宮中勢力と政治』（吉川弘文館、2009年） ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1）
== 脚注 == <references />
{{Stub}}
{{デフォルトソート:きゆうちゆう}} [[Category:大日本帝国]]

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''宮中'''（きゅうちゅう）とは、[[天皇]]がいる[[宮廷]]の中のこと。後に[[政府]]の中を意味する'''[[府中]]'''が[[国政]]そのものを意味するようになったのに対して、宮廷内部の[[事務]]・[[皇室]]の[[家政]]を意味するようになった。
== 戦前における「宮中」 == [[戦前]]の[[日本]]における[[天皇]]は[[国家]]の最高[[統治]]者であるとともに[[国家神道]]の最高[[祭司]]でもあり、[[皇族]]の[[家長]]でもあった。そのため、[[宮内省]]（[[宮内大臣]]）、[[内大臣府]]（[[内大臣]]）、[[元老]]・[[重臣]]、[[侍従長]]、[[侍従武官長]]・[[枢密顧問官]]などといった天皇の日常及び各職務の遂行のために必要な[[組織 (社会科学)|組織]]・人員が置かれていたが、[[大日本帝国憲法]]において天皇の[[親裁権]]の確立と宮中と府中の分離という原則が並立していた。だが、一方で天皇が有していた[[天皇大権]]の大きさを背景として、国政の重要問題に関して天皇による調整が期待されるようになると、宮中側の人々も天皇制の維持・強化の方向において[[政治]]的な問題にも関与するようになった。特に[[昭和期]]には彼らは「'''宮中グループ'''」もしくは「'''宮廷グループ'''」と呼ばれ、[[日本軍|軍部]]や[[官僚]]などの諸勢力と結びついて国政の動向に重要な影響を与えるようになった。だが、宮中グループも標的とされた[[二・二六事件]]以後は強大化する軍部の力を府中・宮中ともに抑制することが出来なくなったために、彼らと妥協もしくは積極的に加担することで自らの[[地位]]を保持するようになっていった。[[近衛文麿]]や[[木戸幸一]]はその代表的人物とされる。[[戦後]]、天皇から国政の実質的権限が剥奪され、国家神道も解体されたことにより、[[日本国憲法]]が認めた[[国事行為]]を行うのに必要な最低限必要な組織として[[宮内府]]→[[宮内庁]]が設置された。
== 参考文献 == *[[増田知子]]「宮中」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） *[[藤原彰]]「宮中グループ」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6） *[[茶谷誠一]]『昭和戦前期の宮中勢力と政治』（吉川弘文館、2009年） ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1）
{{Stub}}

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''宮中'''（きゅうちゅう）とは、[[皇居]]の中のこと。後に[[政府]]の中を意味する'''[[府中]]'''が[[国政]]そのものを意味するようになったのに対して、宮廷の[[事務]]・[[皇室]]の[[家政]]を意味するようになった。
== 戦前期の「宮中」 == [[戦前]]の[[日本]]における[[天皇]]は[[国家]]の最高[[統治]]者であるとともに[[国家神道]]の最高[[祭司]]でもあり、[[皇族]]の[[家長]]でもあった。そのため、[[宮内省]]（[[宮内大臣]]）、[[内大臣府]]（[[内大臣]]）、[[元老]]・[[重臣]]、[[侍従長]]、[[侍従武官長]]・[[枢密顧問官]]などといった天皇の日常及び各職務の遂行のために必要な[[組織 (社会科学)|組織]]・人員が置かれていたが、[[大日本帝国憲法]]において天皇の[[親裁権]]の確立と宮中と府中の分離という原則が並立していた。だが、一方で天皇が有していた[[天皇大権]]の大きさを背景として、国政の重要問題に関して天皇による調整が期待されるようになると、宮中側の人々も天皇制の維持・強化の方向において[[政治]]的な問題にも関与するようになった。特に[[昭和期]]には彼らは「'''宮中グループ'''」もしくは「'''宮廷グループ'''」と呼ばれ、[[日本軍|軍部]]や[[官僚]]などの諸勢力と結びついて国政の動向に重要な影響を与えるようになった。だが、宮中グループも標的とされた[[二・二六事件]]以後は強大化する軍部の力を府中・宮中ともに抑制することが出来なくなったために、彼らと妥協もしくは積極的に加担することで自らの[[地位]]を保持するようになっていった。[[近衛文麿]]や[[木戸幸一]]はその代表的人物とされる。[[戦後]]、天皇から国政の実質的権限が剥奪され、国家神道も解体されたことにより、[[日本国憲法]]が認めた[[国事行為]]を行うのに必要な最低限必要な組織として[[宮内府]]→[[宮内庁]]が設置された。
== 参考文献 == * [[増田知子]]「宮中」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、{{和暦|1993}}） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） * [[藤原彰]]「宮中グループ」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、{{和暦|2000}}） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6） * [[茶谷誠一]]『昭和戦前期の宮中勢力と政治』（吉川弘文館、{{和暦|2009}}） ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1）
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{デフォルトソート:きゆうちゆう}} [[Category:大日本帝国]] [[Category:日本の皇室]] [[Category:天皇制]] [[Category:戦前日本の政治]] [[Category:宮内庁の歴史]]

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''宮中'''（きゅうちゅう）とは、[[宮殿]]の中を指す語である。とりわけ[[皇居]]のことを指すことが多い<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref>。また[[神社]]の中や境内を指すこともある<ref name="kokugo" />。宮殿の中の意味でつかわれる場合は[[禁中]]や[[禁裏]]と同義である<ref name="kokugo" />。
== 概要 == 主に府中（国政）の対義語として問題になった<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref>。本来[[立憲君主制]]の下では宮中と府中は分離され、国政についての責任機関が確立している必要があるが、明治前期の[[太政官制]]のもとでは[[天皇親政]]下[[太政大臣]]・[[左大臣]]・[[右大臣]]・[[参議]]・[[卿]]などの国政の職位が天皇の家臣(朝臣)としての色彩が強く、しかも従来からの天皇の家臣である[[公家]]出身者がその職につくことが多かったため宮廷事務と国政事務の区別が明確になっていなかった<ref name="sekai" />。
[[大日本帝国憲法|明治憲法]]制定で立憲体制が整えられた後も同憲法に定められた天皇の権限が大きかったため、宮中内の天皇側近者は政治に影響を及ぼしやすかった。これを宮廷グループ、あるいは宮中グループと呼んだ<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref>。[[元老]]、[[内大臣府|内大臣]]、[[宮内大臣]]、[[侍従長]]やその周辺を指し、昭和期には[[西園寺公望]]を旧宮廷グループ、[[近衛文麿]]や[[木戸幸一]]らを新宮廷グループと呼んでいた<ref name="nipo" />。
== 参考文献 == * [[増田知子]]「宮中」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、[[1993年]]（平成5年）） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） * [[藤原彰]]「宮中グループ」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、[[2000年]]（平成12年）） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6） * [[茶谷誠一]]『昭和戦前期の宮中勢力と政治』（吉川弘文館、[[2009年]]（平成21年）） ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1）
== 出典 == <references /> {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{デフォルトソート:きゆうちゆう}} [[Category:日本の皇室]] [[Category:天皇制]] [[Category:戦前日本の政治]] [[Category:宮内庁の歴史]]

[[大日本帝国憲法|明治憲法]]制定で立憲体制が整えられた後も同憲法に定められた天皇の権限が大きかったため、宮中内の天皇側近者は政治に影響を及ぼしやすかった。これを宮廷グループ、あるいは宮中グループと呼んだ<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref>。[[元老]]、[[内大臣府|内大臣]]、[[宮内大臣]]、[[侍従長]]やその周辺を指し、昭和期には[[西園寺公望]]を旧宮廷グループ、[[近衛文麿]]や[[木戸幸一]]らを新宮廷グループと呼んでいた<ref name="nipo" />。

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''宮中'''（きゅうちゅう）とは、[[宮殿]]の中を指す語である。とりわけ[[皇居]]のことを指すことが多い<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref>。また[[神社]]の中や境内を指すこともある<ref name="kokugo" />。宮殿の中の意味でつかわれる場合は[[禁中]]や[[禁裏]]と同義である<ref name="kokugo" />。
== 概要 == 主に府中（国政）の対義語として問題になった<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref>。本来[[立憲君主制]]の下では宮中と府中は分離され、国政についての責任機関が確立している必要があるが、明治前期の[[太政官制]]のもとでは[[天皇親政]]下[[太政大臣]]・[[左大臣]]・[[右大臣]]・[[参議]]・[[卿]]などの国政の職位が天皇の家臣(朝臣)としての色彩が強く、しかも従来からの天皇の家臣である[[公家]]出身者がその職につくことが多かったため宮廷事務と国政事務の区別が明確になっていなかった<ref name="sekai" />。
[[大日本帝国憲法|明治憲法]]制定で立憲体制が整えられた後も同憲法に定められた天皇の権限が大きかったため、宮中内の天皇側近者は政治に影響を及ぼしやすかった。これを宮廷グループ、あるいは宮中グループと呼んだ<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref>。[[元老]]、[[内大臣府|内大臣]]、[[宮内大臣]]、[[侍従長]]やその周辺を指し、昭和期には[[西園寺公望]]を旧宮廷グループ、[[近衛文麿]]や[[木戸幸一]]らを新宮廷グループと呼んでいた<ref name="nipo" />。
== 参考文献 == * [[増田知子]]「宮中」（『[[日本史大事典]] 2』（[[平凡社]]、[[1993年]]（平成5年）） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） * [[藤原彰]]「宮中グループ」（『[[日本歴史大事典]] 1』（[[小学館]]、[[2000年]]（平成12年）） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6） * [[茶谷誠一]]『昭和戦前期の宮中勢力と政治』（[[吉川弘文館]]、[[2009年]]（平成21年）） ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1）
== 出典 == <references /> {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{デフォルトソート:きゆうちゆう}} [[Category:日本の皇室]] [[Category:天皇制]] [[Category:戦前日本の政治]] [[Category:宮内庁の歴史]]

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''宮中'''（きゅうちゅう）とは、[[宮殿]]の中を指す語である。とりわけ[[皇居]]のことを指すことが多い<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref>。昔、中国の王城の門が九つ重なっていたところから、宮中は「九重」と表現されることがある<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref>。また[[神社]]の中や境内を指すこともある<ref name="kokugo" />。宮殿の中の意味でつかわれる場合は[[禁中]]や[[禁裏]]と同義である<ref name="kokugo" />。
== 概要 == 主に府中（国政）の対義語として問題になった<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref>。本来[[立憲君主制]]の下では宮中と府中は分離され、国政についての責任機関が確立している必要があるが、明治前期の[[太政官制]]のもとでは[[天皇親政]]下[[太政大臣]]・[[左大臣]]・[[右大臣]]・[[参議]]・[[卿]]などの国政の職位が天皇の家臣(朝臣)としての色彩が強く、しかも従来からの天皇の家臣である[[公家]]出身者がその職につくことが多かったため宮廷事務と国政事務の区別が明確になっていなかった<ref name="sekai" />。
[[大日本帝国憲法|明治憲法]]制定で立憲体制が整えられた後も同憲法に定められた天皇の権限が大きかったため、宮中内の天皇側近者は政治に影響を及ぼしやすかった。これを宮廷グループ、あるいは宮中グループと呼んだ<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref>。[[元老]]、[[内大臣府|内大臣]]、[[宮内大臣]]、[[侍従長]]やその周辺を指し、昭和期には[[西園寺公望]]を旧宮廷グループ、[[近衛文麿]]や[[木戸幸一]]らを新宮廷グループと呼んでいた<ref name="nipo" />。
== 参考文献 == * [[増田知子]]「宮中」（『[[日本史大事典]] 2』（[[平凡社]]、[[1993年]]（平成5年）） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） * [[藤原彰]]「宮中グループ」（『[[日本歴史大事典]] 1』（[[小学館]]、[[2000年]]（平成12年）） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6） * [[茶谷誠一]]『昭和戦前期の宮中勢力と政治』（[[吉川弘文館]]、[[2009年]]（平成21年）） ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1）
== 出典 == <references /> {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{デフォルトソート:きゆうちゆう}} [[Category:日本の皇室]] [[Category:天皇制]] [[Category:戦前日本の政治]] [[Category:宮内庁の歴史]]
[[ja:宮中]]

[[ja:宮中]] {{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of the [[palace]], often specifically denoting the [[Imperial Palace]] of Japan<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref>. Historically, the term "Kyūchū" has also been expressed as "Kokonoe" (九重), originating from the nine gates of the royal city in ancient China<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref>. Additionally, it may refer to the interior or precincts of a [[shrine]]<ref name="kokugo" />. When used in the context of the palace interior, it is synonymous with [[Kinchū]] (禁中) and [[Kinri]] (禁裏)<ref name="kokugo" />.
== Overview ==
The term was primarily contrasted with Fuchū (府中), which refers to national administration<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref>. Under a constitutional monarchy, Kyūchū and Fuchū are supposed to remain separate, with clear responsibility mechanisms established for national governance. However, during the early [[Meiji period]] under the [[Daijōkan]] system, [[direct imperial rule]] meant that positions such as [[Daijō Daijin]] (太政大臣), [[Sadaijin]] (左大臣), [[Udaijin]] (右大臣), [[Sangi]] (参議), and [[Kyō]] (卿) bore a strong resemblance to retainers (朝臣) of the Emperor. Moreover, traditional courtiers ([[Kuge]]) often held these positions, blurring the lines between palace and state matters<ref name="sekai" />.
Even after the establishment of a constitutional system under the [[Meiji Constitution]], the substantial authority granted to the Emperor led to the influence of palace insiders on politics. This group, referred to as the "Court Group" or "Kyūchū Group," included [[Genrō]], the [[Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Privy Seal]], the [[Minister of the Imperial Household]], and the [[Grand Chamberlain]]<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref>. During the [[Shōwa era]], the "Old Court Group" included figures like [[Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "New Court Group" comprised individuals such as [[Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[Koichi Kido]]<ref name="nipo" />.
== References ==
* [[Masuda Tomoko]] "Kyūchū" in *[[Nihonshi Daijiten]] 2* (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * [[Fujiwara Akira]] "Kyūchū Group" in *[[Nihon Rekishi Daijiten]] 1* (Shogakukan, 2000) ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * [[Chatani Seiichi]], *Showa Pre-War Court Forces and Politics* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009) ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1
== Notes ==
<references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

[[ja:宮中]]

== Overview ==
The term was primarily contrasted with Fuchū (府中), which refers to national administration<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref>. Under a constitutional monarchy, Kyūchū and Fuchū are supposed to remain separate, with clear responsibility mechanisms established for national governance. However, during the early [[Meiji period]] under the [[Daijōkan]] system, [[direct imperial rule]] meant that positions such as [[Daijō Daijin]] (太政大臣), [[Sadaijin]] (左大臣), [[Udaijin]] (右大臣), [[Sangi]] (参議), and [[Kyō]] (卿) bore a strong resemblance to retainers (朝臣) of the Emperor. Moreover, traditional courtiers ([[Kuge]]) often held these positions, blurring the lines between palace and state matters<ref name="sekai" />.
Even after the establishment of a constitutional system under the [[Meiji Constitution]], the substantial authority granted to the Emperor led to the influence of palace insiders on politics. This group, referred to as the "Court Group" or "Kyūchū Group," included [[Genrō]], the [[Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Privy Seal]], the [[Minister of the Imperial Household]], and the [[Grand Chamberlain]]<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref>. During the [[Shōwa era]], the "Old Court Group" included figures like [[Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "New Court Group" comprised individuals such as [[Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[Koichi Kido]]<ref name="nipo" />.
== References ==
* [[Masuda Tomoko]] "Kyūchū" in *[[Nihonshi Daijiten]] 2* (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * [[Fujiwara Akira]] "Kyūchū Group" in *[[Nihon Rekishi Daijiten]] 1* (Shogakukan, 2000) ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * [[Chatani Seiichi]], *Showa Pre-War Court Forces and Politics* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009) ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1
== Notes ==
<references />

revision.text:
[[ja:宮中]] {{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of the [[palace]], often specifically denoting the [[Imperial Palace]] of Japan<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref>. Historically, the term "Kyūchū" has also been expressed as "Kokonoe" (九重), originating from the nine gates of the royal city in ancient China<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref>. Additionally, it may refer to the interior or precincts of a [[shrine]]<ref name="kokugo" />. When used in the context of the palace interior, it is synonymous with [[Kinchū]] (禁中) and [[Kinri]] (禁裏)<ref name="kokugo" />.
== Overview ==
The term was primarily contrasted with Fuchū (府中), which refers to national administration<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref>. However, during the early [[Meiji period]] under the [[Daijōkan]] system, [[direct imperial rule]] meant that positions such as [[Daijō Daijin]] (太政大臣), [[Sadaijin]] (左大臣), [[Udaijin]] (右大臣), [[Sangi]] (参議), and [[Kyō]] (卿) bore a strong resemblance to retainers (朝臣) of the Emperor. Moreover, traditional courtiers ([[Kuge]]) often held these positions, blurring the lines between palace and state matters<ref name="sekai" />.
Even after the establishment of a constitutional system under the [[Meiji Constitution]], the substantial authority granted to the Emperor led to the influence of palace insiders on politics. This group, referred to as the "Court Group" or "Kyūchū Group," included [[Genrō]], the [[Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Privy Seal]], the [[Minister of the Imperial Household]], and the [[Grand Chamberlain]]<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref>. During the [[Shōwa era]], the "Old Court Group" included figures like [[Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "New Court Group" comprised individuals such as [[Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[Koichi Kido]]<ref name="nipo" />.
== References ==
* [[Masuda Tomoko]] "Kyūchū" in *[[Nihonshi Daijiten]] 2* (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * [[Fujiwara Akira]] "Kyūchū Group" in *[[Nihon Rekishi Daijiten]] 1* (Shogakukan, 2000) ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * [[Chatani Seiichi]], *Showa Pre-War Court Forces and Politics* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009) ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1

This group, referred to as the "Court Group" or "Kyūchū Group," included [[Genrō]], the [[Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Privy Seal]], the [[Minister of the Imperial Household]], and the [[Grand Chamberlain]]<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref>. During the [[Shōwa era]], the "Old Court Group" included figures like [[Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "New Court Group" comprised individuals such as [[Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[Koichi Kido]]<ref name="nipo" />.
== References ==
* [[Masuda Tomoko]] "Kyūchū" in *[[Nihonshi Daijiten]] 2* (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * [[Fujiwara Akira]] "Kyūchū Group" in *[[Nihon Rekishi Daijiten]] 1* (Shogakukan, 2000) ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * [[Chatani Seiichi]], *Showa Pre-War Court Forces and Politics* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009) ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1

[[ja:宮中]] {{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of the [[palace]], often specifically denoting the [[Imperial Palace]] of Japan<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref>. Historically, the term "Kyūchū" has also been expressed as "Kokonoe" (九重), originating from the nine gates of the royal city in ancient China<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref>. Additionally, it may refer to the interior or precincts of a [[shrine]]<ref name="kokugo" />. When used in the context of the palace interior, it is synonymous with [[Kinchū]] (禁中) and [[Kinri]] (禁裏)<ref name="kokugo" />.
== Overview ==
The term was primarily contrasted with Fuchū (府中), which refers to national administration<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref>. Under a constitutional monarchy, Kyūchū and Fuchū are supposed to remain separate, with clear responsibility mechanisms established for national governance. However, during the early [[Meiji period]] under the [[Daijōkan]] system, [[direct imperial rule]] meant that positions such as [[Daijō Daijin]] (太政大臣), [[Sadaijin]] (左大臣), [[Udaijin]] (右大臣), [[Sangi]] (参議), and [[Kyō]] (卿) bore a strong resemblance to retainers (朝臣) of the Emperor. Moreover, traditional courtiers ([[Kuge]]) often held these positions, blurring the lines between palace and state matters<ref name="sekai" />.
Even after the establishment of a constitutional system under the [[Meiji Constitution]], the substantial authority granted to the Emperor led to the influence of palace insiders on politics. This group, referred to as the "Court Group" or "Kyūchū Group," included [[Genrō]], the [[Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Privy Seal]], the [[Minister of the Imperial Household]], and the [[Grand Chamberlain]]<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref>. During the [[Shōwa era]], the "Old Court Group" included figures like [[Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "New Court Group" comprised individuals such as [[Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[Koichi Kido]]<ref name="nipo" />.
== References ==
* [[Masuda Tomoko]] "Kyūchū" in *[[Nihonshi Daijiten]] 2* (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * [[Fujiwara Akira]] "Kyūchū Group" in *[[Nihon Rekishi Daijiten]] 1* (Shogakukan, 2000) ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * [[Chatani Seiichi]], *Showa Pre-War Court Forces and Politics* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009) ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1
== Notes ==

* [[Masuda Tomoko]] "Kyūchū" in *[[Nihonshi Daijiten]] 2* (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * [[Fujiwara Akira]] "Kyūchū Group" in *[[Nihon Rekishi Daijiten]] 1* (Shogakukan, 2000) ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * [[Chatani Seiichi]], *Showa Pre-War Court Forces and Politics* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009) ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|Fuchū|ja|府中}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />

These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />

Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kyūchū-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kyūchū-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kyūchū-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kyūchū-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kyūchū-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kyūchū-qq]] [[Category:History of the Imperial Household Agency]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:宮中]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:ppp]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]]

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qq deprecated open]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
