[[ja:宮中]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:yūchū]][[Category:qq]]

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

[[ja:宮中]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:yūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kyūchū}}

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

[[ja:宮中]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:yūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kyūchū}}

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:Completion tier 1]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

[[ja:宮中]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:yūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kyūchū}}

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

[[ja:宮中]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:yūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kyūchū}}[[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kyūchū}}

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 2]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

[[ja:宮中]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:yūchū]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kyūchū}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kyūchū]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kyūchū}} [[Category:New]]

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]] on (CatAlot)
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿|12=draft}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居|12=draft}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=draft}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū|12=draft}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制|12=draft}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=draft}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣|12=draft}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|12=draft}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法|12=draft}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=draft}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|12=draft}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿|12=draft}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一|12=draft}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|12=draft}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=draft}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰|12=draft}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=draft}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|12=draft}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|12=draft}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿|12=draft}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一|12=draft}}.<ref name="nipo" />

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿|12=draft}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居|12=draft}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=draft}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū|12=draft}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制|12=draft}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=draft}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣|12=draft}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|12=draft}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法|12=draft}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=draft}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|12=draft}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿|12=draft}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一|12=draft}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|12=draft}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=draft}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰|12=draft}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=draft}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|12=draft}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣|12=draft}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|12=draft}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|12=draft}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿|12=draft}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一|12=draft}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|12=draft}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=draft}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰|12=draft}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=draft}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|12=draft}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''宮中'''（きゅうちゅう）とは、[[宮殿]]の中を指す語である。とりわけ[[皇居]]のことを指すことが多い<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref>。昔、中国の王城の門が九つ重なっていたところから、宮中は「九重」と表現されることがある<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref>。また[[神社]]の中や境内を指すこともある<ref name="kokugo" />。宮殿の中の意味でつかわれる場合は[[禁中]]や[[禁裏]]と同義である<ref name="kokugo" />。
== 概要 == 主に府中（国政）の対義語として問題になった<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref>。本来[[立憲君主制]]の下では宮中と府中は分離され、国政についての責任機関が確立している必要があるが、明治前期の[[太政官制]]のもとでは[[天皇親政]]下[[太政大臣]]・[[左大臣]]・[[右大臣]]・[[参議]]・[[卿]]などの国政の職位が天皇の家臣(朝臣)としての色彩が強く、しかも従来からの天皇の家臣である[[公家]]出身者がその職につくことが多かったため宮廷事務と国政事務の区別が明確になっていなかった<ref name="sekai" />。
[[大日本帝国憲法|明治憲法]]制定で立憲体制が整えられた後も同憲法に定められた天皇の権限が大きかったため、宮中内の天皇側近者は政治に影響を及ぼしやすかった。これを宮廷グループ、あるいは宮中グループと呼んだ<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref>。[[元老]]、[[内大臣府|内大臣]]、[[宮内大臣]]、[[侍従長]]やその周辺を指し、昭和期には[[西園寺公望]]を旧宮廷グループ、[[近衛文麿]]や[[木戸幸一]]らを新宮廷グループと呼んでいた<ref name="nipo" />。
== 参考文献 == * [[増田知子]]「宮中」（『[[日本史大事典]] 2』（[[平凡社]]、[[1993年]]（平成5年）） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） * [[藤原彰]]「宮中グループ」（『[[日本歴史大事典]] 1』（[[小学館]]、[[2000年]]（平成12年）） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6） * [[茶谷誠一]]『昭和戦前期の宮中勢力と政治』（[[吉川弘文館]]、[[2009年]]（平成21年）） ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1）
== 出典 == <references /> {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{デフォルトソート:きゆうちゆう}} [[Category:日本の皇室]] [[Category:天皇制]] [[Category:戦前日本の政治]] [[Category:宮内庁の歴史]]

{{Otheruses|宮中}} '''宮中'''（きゅうちゅう）とは、[[宮殿]]の中を指す語である。とりわけ[[皇居]]のことを指すことが多い<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref>。昔、中国の王城の門が九つ重なっていたところから、宮中は「九重」と表現されることがある<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref>。また[[神社]]の中や境内を指すこともある<ref name="kokugo" />。宮殿の中の意味でつかわれる場合は[[禁中]]や[[禁裏]]と同義である<ref name="kokugo" />。
== 概要 == 主に府中（国政）の対義語として問題になった<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref>。本来[[立憲君主制]]の下では宮中と府中は分離され、国政についての責任機関が確立している必要があるが、明治前期の[[太政官制]]のもとでは[[天皇親政]]下[[太政大臣]]・[[左大臣]]・[[右大臣]]・[[参議]]・[[卿]]などの国政の職位が天皇の家臣(朝臣)としての色彩が強く、しかも従来からの天皇の家臣である[[公家]]出身者がその職につくことが多かったため宮廷事務と国政事務の区別が明確になっていなかった<ref name="sekai" />。
[[大日本帝国憲法|明治憲法]]制定で立憲体制が整えられた後も同憲法に定められた天皇の権限が大きかったため、宮中内の天皇側近者は政治に影響を及ぼしやすかった。これを宮廷グループ、あるいは宮中グループと呼んだ<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref>。[[元老]]、[[内大臣府|内大臣]]、[[宮内大臣]]、[[侍従長]]やその周辺を指し、昭和期には[[西園寺公望]]を旧宮廷グループ、[[近衛文麿]]や[[木戸幸一]]らを新宮廷グループと呼んでいた<ref name="nipo" />。
== 参考文献 == * [[増田知子]]「宮中」（『[[日本史大事典]] 2』（[[平凡社]]、[[1993年]]（平成5年）） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） * [[藤原彰]]「宮中グループ」（『[[日本歴史大事典]] 1』（[[小学館]]、[[2000年]]（平成12年）） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6） * [[茶谷誠一]]『昭和戦前期の宮中勢力と政治』（[[吉川弘文館]]、[[2009年]]（平成21年）） ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1）
== 出典 == <references /> {{Japanese-history-stub}}

== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/府中|qq=}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/constitutional monarchy|qq=}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|qq=}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō Daijin|qq=}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadaijin|qq=}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udaijin|qq=}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|qq=}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Constitution|qq=}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genrō|qq=}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|qq=}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|qq=}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saionji Kinmochi|qq=}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumimaro Konoe|qq=}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Kido|qq=}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|qq=}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha|qq=}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akira Fujiwara|qq=}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogakukan|qq=}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|qq=}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|qq=}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
