revision.text:
'''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|palace|ja|宮殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/palace|qq=}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|qq=}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|qq=}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|qq=}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|ja|府中|府中=府中|lt=Fuchū|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/府中|qq=}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|constitutional monarchy|ja|立憲君主制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/constitutional monarchy|qq=}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|qq=}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō Daijin|qq=}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadaijin|qq=}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udaijin|qq=}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議|1=Sangi (Japan)|lt=Sangi|2=ja|3=参議|4=ru|5=Санги|6=fr|7=Sangi_(Japon)|8=ko|9=참의_(일본사)|10=es|11=Sangi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|qq=}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|大日本帝国憲法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Constitution|qq=}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|ja|元老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genrō|qq=}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|ja|内大臣府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|qq=}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|qq=}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saionji Kinmochi|qq=}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumimaro Konoe|qq=}} and {{ill|Koichi Kido|ja|木戸幸一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Kido|qq=}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|qq=}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha|qq=}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|ja|藤原彰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akira Fujiwara|qq=}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogakukan|qq=}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|qq=}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|qq=}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

Positions such as {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō Daijin|qq=}}, {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣|1=Minister of the Left|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadaijin|qq=}}, {{ill|Udaijin|ja|右大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udaijin|qq=}}, {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議|1=Sangi (Japan)|lt=Sangi|2=ja|3=参議|4=ru|5=Санги|6=fr|7=Sangi_(Japon)|8=ko|9=참의_(일본사)|10=es|11=Sangi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|qq=}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. revision.text:
'''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a [[:en:Palace|palace]], often specifically denoting the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]].<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|qq=}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|qq=}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to [[:en:府中|Fuchū]] (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a [[:en:Constitutional monarchy|constitutional monarchy]], Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|qq=}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]], [[:en:Minister of the Left|Minister of the Left]], [[:en:Minister of the Right|Udaijin]], [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Sangi]], and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|qq=}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|qq=}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by [[:en:Saionji Kinmochi|Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "new court group" included figures such as [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[:en:Kōichi Kido|Koichi Kido]].<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|qq=}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * [[:en:Akira Fujiwara|Akira Fujiwara]], "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|qq=}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|qq=}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:宮内庁の歴史
revision.text:
'''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a [[:en:Palace|palace]], often specifically denoting the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]].<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|qq=}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|qq=}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to [[:en:府中|Fuchū]] (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a [[:en:Constitutional monarchy|constitutional monarchy]], Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|qq=}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]], [[:en:Minister of the Left|Minister of the Left]], [[:en:Minister of the Right|Udaijin]], [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Sangi]], and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|qq=}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|qq=}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by [[:en:Saionji Kinmochi|Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "new court group" included figures such as [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[:en:Kōichi Kido|Koichi Kido]].<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|qq=}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * [[:en:Akira Fujiwara|Akira Fujiwara]], "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|qq=}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|qq=}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|宮中|version=95582023|comment=Imported full ja history}}

These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by [[:en:Saionji Kinmochi|Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "new court group" included figures such as [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[:en:Kōichi Kido|Koichi Kido]].<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|qq=}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * [[:en:Akira Fujiwara|Akira Fujiwara]], "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|qq=}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|qq=}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

revision.text:
'''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a [[:en:Palace|palace]], often specifically denoting the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]].<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|qq=}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to [[:en:府中|Fuchū]] (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a [[:en:Constitutional monarchy|constitutional monarchy]], Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]], [[:en:Minister of the Left|Minister of the Left]], [[:en:Minister of the Right|Udaijin]], [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Sangi]], and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|qq=}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by [[:en:Saionji Kinmochi|Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "new court group" included figures such as [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[:en:Kōichi Kido|Koichi Kido]].<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|qq=}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * [[:en:Akira Fujiwara|Akira Fujiwara]], "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|qq=}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|qq=}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従}}, along with their associates. {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11453221]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a [[:en:Palace|palace]], often specifically denoting the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]].<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|qq=|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to [[:en:府中|Fuchū]] (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a [[:en:Constitutional monarchy|constitutional monarchy]], Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]], [[:en:Minister of the Left|Minister of the Left]], [[:en:Minister of the Right|Udaijin]], [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Sangi]], and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|qq=|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by [[:en:Saionji Kinmochi|Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "new court group" included figures such as [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[:en:Kōichi Kido|Koichi Kido]].<ref name="nipo" />

== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to [[:en:府中|Fuchū]] (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a [[:en:Constitutional monarchy|constitutional monarchy]], Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]], [[:en:Minister of the Left|Minister of the Left]], [[:en:Minister of the Right|Udaijin]], [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Sangi]], and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|qq=|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by [[:en:Saionji Kinmochi|Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "new court group" included figures such as [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[:en:Kōichi Kido|Koichi Kido]].<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|qq=|1=Tomoko Masuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q64783120}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * [[:en:Akira Fujiwara|Akira Fujiwara]], "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|qq=|1=Seiichi Chadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933143}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|qq=|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]], [[:en:Minister of the Left|Minister of the Left]], [[:en:Minister of the Right|Udaijin]], [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Sangi]], and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|qq=|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. == References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|qq=|1=Tomoko Masuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q64783120}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * [[:en:Akira Fujiwara|Akira Fujiwara]], "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|qq=|1=Seiichi Chadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933143}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|qq=|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11453221]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a [[:en:Palace|palace]], often specifically denoting the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]].<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to [[:en:府中|Fuchū]] (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a [[:en:Constitutional monarchy|constitutional monarchy]], Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]], [[:en:Minister of the Left|Minister of the Left]], [[:en:Minister of the Right|Udaijin]], [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Sangi]], and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by [[:en:Saionji Kinmochi|Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "new court group" included figures such as [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[:en:Kōichi Kido|Koichi Kido]].<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|1=Tomoko Masuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q64783120}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * [[:en:Akira Fujiwara|Akira Fujiwara]], "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|1=Seiichi Chadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933143}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]], [[:en:Minister of the Left|Minister of the Left]], [[:en:Minister of the Right|Udaijin]], [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Sangi]], and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by [[:en:Saionji Kinmochi|Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "new court group" included figures such as [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[:en:Kōichi Kido|Koichi Kido]].<ref name="nipo" />

<!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--><!--Kyūchū--> [[Category:History of the Imperial Household Agency]] [[Category:Politics of the Empire of Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11453221}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11453221]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a [[:en:Palace|palace]], often specifically denoting the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]].<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to [[:en:府中|Fuchū]] (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a [[:en:Constitutional monarchy|constitutional monarchy]], Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]], [[:en:Minister of the Left|Minister of the Left]], [[:en:Minister of the Right|Udaijin]], [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Sangi]], and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by [[:en:Saionji Kinmochi|Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "new court group" included figures such as [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[:en:Kōichi Kido|Koichi Kido]].<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|1=Tomoko Masuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q64783120}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * [[:en:Akira Fujiwara|Akira Fujiwara]], "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|1=Seiichi Chadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933143}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. == References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|1=Tomoko Masuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q64783120}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * [[:en:Akira Fujiwara|Akira Fujiwara]], "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|1=Seiichi Chadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933143}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11453221]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a [[:en:Palace|palace]], often specifically denoting the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]].<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to [[:en:府中|Fuchū]] (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a [[:en:Constitutional monarchy|constitutional monarchy]], Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]], [[:en:Minister of the Left|Minister of the Left]], [[:en:Minister of the Right|Udaijin]], [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Sangi]], and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by [[:en:Saionji Kinmochi|Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "new court group" included figures such as [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[:en:Kōichi Kido|Koichi Kido]].<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|1=Tomoko Masuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q64783120}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * [[:en:Akira Fujiwara|Akira Fujiwara]], "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|1=Seiichi Chadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933143}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11453221]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a [[:en:Palace|palace]], often specifically denoting the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]].<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to [[:en:府中|Fuchū]] (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a [[:en:Constitutional monarchy|constitutional monarchy]], Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]], [[:en:Minister of the Left|Minister of the Left]], [[:en:Minister of the Right|Udaijin]], [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Sangi]], and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]] and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the [[:en:Genrō|Genrō]], the [[:en:Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan]], the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by [[:en:Saionji Kinmochi|Saionji Kinmochi]], while the "new court group" included figures such as [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and [[:en:Kōichi Kido|Koichi Kido]].<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|1=Tomoko Masuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q64783120}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * [[:en:Akira Fujiwara|Akira Fujiwara]], "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|1=Seiichi Chadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933143}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|宮中|version=95582023|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| }}

== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|en|府中|WD=Q122148318|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|Constitutional monarchy|en|Constitutional monarchy|WD=Q41614|lt=constitutional monarchy}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō Daijin}}, {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left}}, {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Udaijin}}, {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Sangi}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|en|Genrō|WD=Q254907|lt=Genrō}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|en|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|WD=Q712143|lt=Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|en|Saionji Kinmochi|WD=Q297628|lt=Saionji Kinmochi}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe}} and {{ill|Kōichi Kido|en|Kōichi Kido|WD=Q699712|lt=Koichi Kido}}.<ref name="nipo" />

These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|en|Genrō|WD=Q254907|lt=Genrō}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|en|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|WD=Q712143|lt=Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|en|Saionji Kinmochi|WD=Q297628|lt=Saionji Kinmochi}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe}} and {{ill|Kōichi Kido|en|Kōichi Kido|WD=Q699712|lt=Koichi Kido}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|1=Tomoko Masuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q64783120}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|en|Akira Fujiwara|WD=Q4701129|lt=Akira Fujiwara}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|en|Shogakukan|WD=Q785697|lt=Shogakukan}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|1=Seiichi Chadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933143}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]]
'''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|Palace|en|Palace|WD=Q16560|lt=palace}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|en|Tokyo Imperial Palace|WD=Q500681|lt=Imperial Palace}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|en|府中|WD=Q122148318|lt=Fuchū}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|Constitutional monarchy|en|Constitutional monarchy|WD=Q41614|lt=constitutional monarchy}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō Daijin}}, {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left}}, {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Udaijin}}, {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Sangi}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|en|Genrō|WD=Q254907|lt=Genrō}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|en|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|WD=Q712143|lt=Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|en|Saionji Kinmochi|WD=Q297628|lt=Saionji Kinmochi}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe}} and {{ill|Kōichi Kido|en|Kōichi Kido|WD=Q699712|lt=Koichi Kido}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|1=Tomoko Masuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q64783120}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|en|Akira Fujiwara|WD=Q4701129|lt=Akira Fujiwara}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|en|Shogakukan|WD=Q785697|lt=Shogakukan}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|1=Seiichi Chadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933143}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮中]]
'''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|Palace|en|Palace|WD=Q16560|lt=palace|1=Palace}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|en|Tokyo Imperial Palace|WD=Q500681|lt=Imperial Palace|1=Tokyo Imperial Palace}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|en|府中|WD=Q122148318|lt=Fuchū|1=府中}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|Constitutional monarchy|en|Constitutional monarchy|WD=Q41614|lt=constitutional monarchy|1=Constitutional monarchy}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō Daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}, {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left|1=Minister of the Left}}, {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Udaijin|1=Minister of the Right}}, {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Sangi|1=Sangi (Japan)}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|en|Genrō|WD=Q254907|lt=Genrō|1=Genrō}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|en|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|WD=Q712143|lt=Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|1=Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|en|Saionji Kinmochi|WD=Q297628|lt=Saionji Kinmochi|1=Saionji Kinmochi}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe|1=Fumimaro Konoe}} and {{ill|Kōichi Kido|en|Kōichi Kido|WD=Q699712|lt=Koichi Kido|1=Kōichi Kido}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|1=Tomoko Masuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q64783120}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|en|Akira Fujiwara|WD=Q4701129|lt=Akira Fujiwara|1=Akira Fujiwara}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488|1=Nihon Rekishi Daijiten}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|en|Shogakukan|WD=Q785697|lt=Shogakukan|1=Shogakukan}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|1=Seiichi Chadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933143}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />

However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. '''Kyūchū''' (宮中) refers to the interior of a {{ill|Palace|en|Palace|qid=Q16560|lt=palace|1=Palace}}, often specifically denoting the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|en|Tokyo Imperial Palace|qid=Q500681|lt=Imperial Palace|1=Tokyo Imperial Palace}}.<ref name="kokugo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 精選版 日本国語大辞典「宮中」]</ref> Historically, because the gates of Chinese royal castles were structured in nine successive layers, the term Kyūchū has sometimes been expressed as "Kyūjū" (九重).<ref>{{Kotobank|九重}}</ref> Additionally, Kyūchū can also refer to the interior or precincts of a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}.<ref name="kokugo" /> When used in the sense of the interior of a palace, it is synonymous with {{ill|Kinchū|ja|禁中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinchū|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591117}} and {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591117}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
== Overview == Kyūchū became a key concept as the counterpart to {{ill|府中|en|府中|qid=Q122148318|lt=Fuchū|1=府中}} (state administration).<ref name="sekai" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E4%B8%AD-477175 世界大百科事典 第２版「宮中」]</ref> Under a {{ill|Constitutional monarchy|en|Constitutional monarchy|qid=Q41614|lt=constitutional monarchy|1=Constitutional monarchy}}, Kyūchū and Fuchū are meant to be distinct, with clear institutional responsibility for national governance. However, during the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Daijōkan system|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125768223}}, the distinction between palace affairs and state administration was unclear. Positions such as {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō Daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}, {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|qid=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left|1=Minister of the Left}}, {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|qid=Q1351174|lt=Udaijin|1=Minister of the Right}}, {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|qid=Q1133663|lt=Sangi|1=Sangi (Japan)}}, and {{ill|Kyō|ja|卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō|1=Kyou (officials)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10910812}} were viewed as courtly roles under the emperor, rather than as independent state officials. These roles were often filled by court nobles (kuge) from traditional families, further blending the lines between courtly and governmental duties.<ref name="sekai" />
Even after the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|qid=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}} and its constitutional system, the significant powers granted to the emperor allowed his close advisors within Kyūchū to wield political influence. These close associates were referred to as the "court group" or "Kyūchū group."<ref name="nipo" >[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%AE%E5%BB%B7%E3%82%B0%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97-1524205 日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)「宮廷グループ」]</ref> This group included figures such as the {{ill|Genrō|en|Genrō|qid=Q254907|lt=Genrō|1=Genrō}}, the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|en|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|qid=Q712143|lt=Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|1=Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan}}, the {{ill|Minister of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内大臣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of the Imperial Household|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内省|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8193428}}, and the {{ill|Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja|侍従長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grand Chamberlain of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2948088}}, along with their associates. During the Shōwa period, the "old court group" was represented by {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|en|Saionji Kinmochi|qid=Q297628|lt=Saionji Kinmochi|1=Saionji Kinmochi}}, while the "new court group" included figures such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|qid=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe|1=Fumimaro Konoe}} and {{ill|Kōichi Kido|en|Kōichi Kido|qid=Q699712|lt=Koichi Kido|1=Kōichi Kido}}.<ref name="nipo" />
== References == * {{ill|Tomoko Masuda|ja|増田知子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomoko Masuda|1=Tomoko Masuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q64783120}}, "Kyūchū," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|qid=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * {{ill|Akira Fujiwara|en|Akira Fujiwara|qid=Q4701129|lt=Akira Fujiwara|1=Akira Fujiwara}}, "Court Group," in *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524488|1=Nihon Rekishi Daijiten}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|en|Shogakukan|qid=Q785697|lt=Shogakukan|1=Shogakukan}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6. * {{ill|Seiichi Chatani|ja|茶谷誠一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiichi Chatani|1=Seiichi Chadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q30933143}}, *Court Influence and Politics During the Pre-War Shōwa Period* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4346755}}, 2009), ISBN 978-4-642-03792-1.
== Notes == <references />
